write.prompt: Explain "Leadership" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Leadership without authority is getting people aligned on an outcome when you cannot order them to do it

Explain "Conflict Resolution" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Conflict means information surfaced — two people disagree about something that was probably already there
Explain "Decision Making" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Decisions are bets you make with incomplete information
Explain "Collaboration" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Collaboration means making the other person's job easier than they were expecting
Explain "Failure & Learning" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A real failure cost something you cannot get back

Explain "Communication" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Good communication is audience-first: after listening, the other person's next decision should be easier
Explain "Prioritization" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Prioritization is saying no to good things, not bad ones

Explain "Innovation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Most real innovation comes from refusing to accept a tradeoff that everyone else is taking for granted
Explain "Customer Obsession" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Customer obsession is not saying yes to whatever they ask
Explain "Ownership" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Ownership means closing the loop, not playing the hero
Explain "Positioning" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Positioning answers two questions: "for whom" and "compared to what alternative
Explain "Landing Pages" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A landing page has one job: get the right visitor to take one action

Explain "SEO" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: SEO is matching what people are searching for and earning the trust to rank for it through good content and credible links

Explain "Product Analytics" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Good analytics looks like a small tree: one north-star metric that captures the value users get, two or three input metrics that drive it, and one guardrail you must not regress on
Explain "Vectors & Vector Spaces" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A vector is a point and an arrow

write.prompt: Explain "Matrices & Linear Transformations" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A matrix is a machine that takes vectors in and pushes vectors out

Explain "Eigenvalues & Matrix Decomposition" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Most matrices scramble space, but eigenvectors are the special directions that only get stretched, not rotated
Explain "Probability Fundamentals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Probability is a bookkeeping system for uncertainty
Explain "Random Variables & Distributions" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A random variable is a function from outcomes to numbers

problem.title: Expectation and variance of a discrete RV

write.prompt: Explain "Descriptive Statistics" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Descriptive stats compress a dataset into a few numbers you can reason about

Explain "Estimation & Confidence Intervals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A confidence interval is a procedure, not a probability statement about one interval
Explain "Hypothesis Testing" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Hypothesis testing asks: 'how surprising is this data if nothing real changed

Explain "Linear Regression" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Linear regression finds the best linear predictor — geometrically, it projects the target vector onto the column space of your features
Explain "Derivatives & Gradients" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: The gradient points uphill steepest

Explain "Multivariable Optimization" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Convex functions have one bowl-shaped minimum — gradient descent cannot get stuck
Explain "Information & Entropy" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Entropy measures average surprise of a distribution
Explain "Classical Distributions" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Distributions are reusable uncertainty templates
Explain "Sampling & the Central Limit Theorem" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Your data is one draw from a bigger game
Explain "A/B Testing for Engineers" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: An A/B test is a hypothesis test with a deployment budget

write.prompt: Explain "Bayesian Inference" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Frequentist stats ask how surprising the data is under a null

Explain "Maximum Likelihood Estimation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: MLE picks the parameter values that maximize the probability of what you actually saw
Explain "Covariance & Correlation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Covariance is signed co-fluctuation with units

Explain "Bias, Variance & Overfitting" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Bias is systematic error from too-simple a model; variance is sensitivity to which sample you drew
